summary: The 16th President of the United States (1861-1865), who led the Union through the Civil War, delivered the Gettysburg Address and two famous inaugural addresses, suspended the writ of habeas corpus, and issued the Emancipation Proclamation before being assassinated by John Wilkes Booth at Ford's Theatre. Rising from a self-mythologized "Honest Abe" frontier lawyer in Illinois, he rose to national prominence through his 1858 debates against Stephen Douglas and his 1860 Cooper Union speech, married Mary Todd, and has since become the single most memorialized and mythologized figure in American culture, the subject of Walt Whitman's and Vachel Lindsay's elegiac poems, George Saunders's novel Lincoln in the Bardo, Robert Sherwood's play Abe Lincoln in Illinois, Suzan-Lori Parks's play Topdog/Underdog, the Lincoln Memorial, and countless statues and coins.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Lincoln is identified in giveaways: the 16th President of the United States, who led the nation through the Civil War until his 1865 assassination by John Wilkes Booth. He is popularly nicknamed "Honest Abe" and "the Great Emancipator."

works.name: Early Life and Political Rise
works.description: Lincoln's early life was heavily mythologized: the nickname "Honest Abe" arose from stories of him walking miles to return shortchanged cash while working at a general store in Illinois, John Locke Scripps popularized depictions of him carrying rail fences and an axe, and Alexander McClure and Horatio Alger collected folklore about him. His only military service came as a volunteer militia captain during the Black Hawk War. As a circuit-riding lawyer, he intentionally spent months each year away from his wife, according to Dale Carnegie's biography, and he is the only U.S. president to hold a patent, for an invention to buoy grounded vessels over shoals that he was inspired to design after the steamboat Canada ran aground.

works.name: The Lincoln-Douglas Debates and the Road to the Presidency
works.description: Lincoln rose to national prominence through his seven 1858 debates against incumbent Democratic Senator Stephen Douglas, which began in Ottawa and ended in Alton, Illinois, and were covered by reporters using Pitman shorthand; at the second debate, held in Freeport, Douglas laid out his "Freeport Doctrine." During the campaign Lincoln declared "A house divided against itself cannot stand," but he lost the Senate race to Douglas. His national profile rose further with his February 1860 Cooper Union speech in New York, in which he argued that the Constitution allowed the federal government to restrict the expansion of slavery, and with his earlier Lyceum Address, delivered after a mob lynching in St. Louis, which argued that rebuilding the "pillar of liberty" required "the solid quarry of sober reason." He won the presidency in the Election of 1860, defeating Douglas and John Bell.

works.name: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause
works.description: As president, Lincoln suspended the writ of habeas corpus in 1861 to allow the arbitrary arrest of Southern sympathizers, an action taken eight days after Baltimore's Pratt Street Riots and used to arrest that city's mayor and city council; Roger Taney ruled it unconstitutional in Ex parte Merryman, Lincoln defended it in a letter to Matthew Birchard, and an 1863 statute codified it, allowing Ambrose Burnside to arrest Ohio Copperhead Clement Vallandigham (Francis Scott Key's grandson Frank Key Howard was imprisoned in Fort McHenry for criticizing it, and Mark E. Neely's Pulitzer-winning history The Fate of Liberty called it "well-intentioned"). He commuted the death sentences of 264 Dakota after the 1862 Dakota War but approved the execution of the remaining 38, the largest mass execution in American history, and signed the first Homestead Act. For four months between the terms of generals-in-chief McClellan and Halleck, Lincoln personally served as direct commander of all Union armies. He delivered a brief Gettysburg Address following a two-hour oration by Edward Everett, and his two inaugural addresses included the phrases "mystic chords of memory" and "the better angels of our nature" (1861) and "with malice toward none, with charity for all" (1865). Historians including Eric Foner, whose book The Fiery Trial chronicles Lincoln's acceptance of abolitionism and issuance of the Emancipation Proclamation, have defended him against Lerone Bennett's 1968 essay asking "Was Lincoln a White Supremacist?"; Doris Kearns Goodwin's Team of Rivals examines his often-fractious relationship with a cabinet full of men who had wanted his job, while his own secretaries John Hay and John George Nicolay wrote an early ten-volume biography of him.

works.name: Assassination and the Body
works.description: Lincoln was shot by John Wilkes Booth at Ford's Theatre during a performance of Our American Cousin, moments after a character delivered the line "you sockdologizing old man-trap." His funeral train retraced the route of his original inauguration from Washington, D.C. to Springfield, Illinois, carrying the remains of his son Willie alongside his own. In 1876, counterfeiter "Big Jim" Kennally plotted to steal his corpse from Oak Ridge Cemetery, planning to bury it in the Indiana Dunes and hold it for ransom, but was foiled by Secret Service informants; John Carroll Power subsequently formed a "Guard of Honor" to protect the body. In 1901, Fleetwood Lindley became the last living person to see the corpse when it was exhumed, finding that its eyebrows had completely vanished.

works.name: Mary Todd Lincoln and the Todd Family
works.description: Lincoln married Mary Todd, who came from a slaveholding Whig family and had earlier turned down a marriage proposal from Stephen Douglas; three of her brothers, Sam, David, and Alexander Todd, served as Confederate officers and died at Shiloh, Vicksburg, and Baton Rouge respectively, while her half-sister Emilie Todd Helm, widow of a Confederate general, was allowed to remain in Washington, D.C. despite refusing to swear a loyalty oath. Mary caused the "Old Clothes Scandal" by selling off her wardrobe to settle scrutinized jewelry debts, and the formerly enslaved seamstress Elizabeth Keckley became her confidante and later wrote a memoir defending her. After Willie's death, Mary held numerous White House seances to try to contact her deceased sons Eddie and Willie, and scandalized Washington by wearing lilac instead of black mourning at a funeral. Following the war, her son Robert Todd Lincoln had her committed to a mental institution after a series of shopping binges, leading her to attempt suicide by overdosing on laudanum; she later received a retrial that overturned her confinement after smuggling a letter to activist Myra Bradwell.

works.name: Memorials and Monuments
works.description: Lincoln is memorialized by the Henry Bacon-designed Lincoln Memorial, which contains a Daniel Chester French sculpture of him seated with his hands on his knees inside a building with 36 Doric columns overlooking a reflecting pool; the site hosted Marian Anderson's landmark 1939 Easter Sunday concert, organized by Secretary of the Interior Harold Ickes after the Daughters of the American Revolution barred her from Constitution Hall, and later Martin Luther King Jr.'s "I Have a Dream" speech. A sculpture of him by Vinnie Ream, the first government commission awarded to a female artist, stands in the Capitol rotunda opposite one of Grant. He also appears on the obverse of United States pennies, designed in part by Victor D. Brenner, and is commemorated by the Lincoln Highway, a transcontinental road roughly paralleled today by US Route 30 and dotted with statues of him, on which a young Dwight Eisenhower once pranked a 1919 military convoy into fearing a Native American attack. The Abraham Lincoln Brigade, an American volunteer unit of the International Brigades in the Spanish Civil War, was also named for him.

works.name: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture
works.description: Lincoln's assassination inspired a cluster of Walt Whitman elegies collected as "Memories of Abraham Lincoln" in Leaves of Grass: "O Captain! My Captain!" mourns him as "fallen cold and dead," "When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd" describes a "western orb sailing the heaven" and a hermit thrush's song, and "Hush'd Be the Camps To-Day," written just days after the assassination, tells soldiers to "drape our war-worn weapons." Vachel Lindsay's "Abraham Lincoln Walks at Midnight" depicts his ghost pacing sleeplessly near the old courthouse until "white peace" is achieved. George Saunders's Booker Prize-winning novel Lincoln in the Bardo, partly inspired by Edgar Lee Masters's ghost-narrated Spoon River Anthology, depicts Lincoln visiting his son Willie's grave while Willie is trapped among ghosts in the Buddhist "bardo." Robert Sherwood's Pulitzer-winning play Abe Lincoln in Illinois, based on Carl Sandburg's biography, dramatizes Lincoln's youthful romance with Ann Rutledge, while Suzan-Lori Parks's Topdog/Underdog centers on two brothers named Lincoln and Booth, with Lincoln working at an arcade doing whiteface impersonations of the president. Cole Escola's 2024 comedy Oh, Mary! depicts Mary Todd Lincoln as a "well-known niche cabaret legend" hiding her husband's homosexuality, and Seth Grahame-Smith's novel Abraham Lincoln, Vampire Hunter reimagines Lincoln's fight against slavery as a literal hunt for vampires. Photographer William Mumler produced a famous fraudulent "spirit photograph" of Lincoln's ghost standing behind Mary Todd, later exposed with help from P.T. Barnum, and Daniel Day-Lewis won an Academy Award for playing Lincoln in Steven Spielberg's 2012 film.

comprehensive_summary: Abraham Lincoln was the 16th President of the United States, leading the Union through the Civil War from 1861 until his 1865 assassination. His early life was heavily mythologized: nicknamed "Honest Abe" for returning shortchanged cash to customers, popularly depicted (via John Locke Scripps) carrying rail fences and an axe, and the subject of folklore collected by Alexander McClure and Horatio Alger. His only military service came as a volunteer militia captain in the Black Hawk War, and as a lawyer he intentionally spent months away from his wife riding a judicial circuit; he is the only U.S. president to hold a patent, for a device to buoy grounded vessels over shoals inspired by the grounding of the steamboat Canada. Lincoln rose to national prominence through his seven 1858 debates against Stephen Douglas — which ran from Ottawa to Alton, Illinois, and featured Douglas's "Freeport Doctrine" — declaring "A house divided against itself cannot stand" despite losing that Senate race; his profile rose further through his 1860 Cooper Union speech arguing the Constitution allowed the federal government to restrict slavery's expansion, and his earlier Lyceum Address invoking the "solid quarry of sober reason." He won the presidency in 1860, defeating Douglas and John Bell. As president, Lincoln suspended the writ of habeas corpus in 1861 (defended in a letter to Matthew Birchard, ruled unconstitutional by Roger Taney in Ex parte Merryman, and codified by an 1863 statute used to arrest Ohio Copperhead Clement Vallandigham), commuted the death sentences of 264 Dakota after the 1862 Dakota War while approving the execution of the remaining 38, signed the first Homestead Act, and personally commanded all Union armies for four months between the terms of McClellan and Halleck. He delivered a brief Gettysburg Address after Edward Everett's lengthy oration, and his two inaugural addresses gave the country "the better angels of our nature" (1861) and "malice toward none, with charity for all" (1865); historians like Eric Foner, author of The Fiery Trial, have defended his record on slavery against critics, and Doris Kearns Goodwin's Team of Rivals examined his relationship with his cabinet. Lincoln was shot by John Wilkes Booth at Ford's Theatre during Our American Cousin; his funeral train retraced his inaugural route to Springfield, Illinois, and his body was later the target of an 1876 grave-robbery plot by counterfeiter "Big Jim" Kennally before John Carroll Power organized a "Guard of Honor" to protect it. He married Mary Todd, whose slaveholding family included Confederate officers and whose life featured the "Old Clothes Scandal," a confidante in the formerly enslaved seamstress Elizabeth Keckley, numerous White House seances to contact her dead sons, and a later confinement to a mental institution by her son Robert that was eventually overturned. Lincoln is commemorated by the Daniel Chester French-sculpted Lincoln Memorial (site of Marian Anderson's 1939 concert and MLK's "I Have a Dream" speech), a Vinnie Ream statue in the Capitol rotunda, the Lincoln penny, the transcontinental Lincoln Highway, and the Spanish Civil War's Abraham Lincoln Brigade. His death and legacy inspired Walt Whitman's elegies ("O Captain! My Captain!," "When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd," "Hush'd Be the Camps To-Day"), Vachel Lindsay's "Abraham Lincoln Walks at Midnight," George Saunders's novel Lincoln in the Bardo, Robert Sherwood's play Abe Lincoln in Illinois, Suzan-Lori Parks's Topdog/Underdog, Cole Escola's Oh, Mary!, Seth Grahame-Smith's Abraham Lincoln, Vampire Hunter, William Mumler's fraudulent Lincoln "spirit photograph," and Daniel Day-Lewis's Oscar-winning film portrayal.
recursive_suggestions: Mary Todd Lincoln — has substantial independent tossup and bonus-part presence (seances, Old Clothes Scandal, institutionalization); strong candidate for her own topic
Stephen Douglas — Lincoln's recurring rival across the debates, Senate race, and Election of 1860; likely warrants (or already has) his own topic
John Wilkes Booth — assassin with recurring independent presence; likely warrants (or already has) his own topic
George Saunders / Lincoln in the Bardo — extremely high recurrence as an independent Literature answerline; likely already has (or warrants) its own Literature topic
